title: AI for Real Estate Sales: From Confusion to Daily Use
promise: Turn AI from a confusing novelty into a practical daily sales assistant.
audience: Realtors who are curious about AI but not yet using it consistently.
objectives: Explain what AI can and cannot do in real estate sales.
Draft better client emails, texts, listing copy, and updates.
Prepare for buyer/seller appointments and common objections.
Build a simple daily AI workflow.
Use AI safely with privacy, accuracy, and Fair Housing guardrails.

slides.title: AI for Real Estate Sales: From Confusion to Daily Use
slides.subtitle: Miami Realtors AI Course Series
bullets: Turn AI from a confusing novelty into a practical daily sales assistant.
Audience: Realtors who are curious about AI but not yet using it consistently.
slides.notes: Welcome the room, set the practical tone, and frame the class as useful even for agents who do not consider themselves technical.

slides.title: The Promise
slides.subtitle: Turn AI from a confusing novelty into a practical daily sales assistant.
bullets: Professional judgment stays in the driver's seat.
slides.notes: Tell attendees they do not need to master every AI tool. The win is leaving with repeatable workflows.

slides.title: Today at a Glance
slides.subtitle: Built for a 2-hour class; core modules total about 150 minutes plus exercises and discussion.
bullets: What AI Can and Cannot Do
AI for Listings, Sellers, and Marketing
slides.notes: Preview the path so attendees know the class will stay concrete and paced.

slides.title: Professional Guardrails
slides.subtitle: AI helps draft and organize. You still verify, edit, and decide.
slides.notes: Set this expectation early so the rest of the class can be practical without sounding reckless.

slides.title: 1. Welcome and AI Reality Check
slides.subtitle: 10 minutes
bullets: AI is useful when agents give it a job, context, and review.
slides.notes: Open with permission to be practical. Joke lightly that AI has confidence issues.

slides.title: AI is useful when agents give it a job, context, and review.
slides.subtitle: Welcome and AI Reality Check
bullets: Not magic: drafting, organizing, rewriting, role-play.
Not a replacement: broker judgment, legal advice, local expertise.
Best first move: give AI one annoying repeatable task.
slides.notes: Talk track: Open with permission to be practical. Joke lightly that AI has confidence issues.

slides.title: Framework
slides.subtitle: Welcome and AI Reality Check
bullets: Not magic: drafting, organizing, rewriting, role-play.
Not a replacement: broker judgment, legal advice, local expertise.
Best first move: give AI one annoying repeatable task.
slides.notes: Walk through the framework quickly, then move to the demo. The framework should feel usable, not academic.

slides.title: Live Practice
slides.subtitle: Show a rough buyer-call note becoming a polished follow-up.
bullets: Ask attendees to name one task they avoid every week.
Debrief: what improved, what still needs human review?
slides.notes: Facilitate the exercise. Invite one example from the room if time allows. Keep the output short and client-safe.

slides.title: 2. What AI Can and Cannot Do
slides.subtitle: 20 minutes
bullets: AI accelerates thinking; it does not verify reality.
slides.notes: Reinforce that the agent's license and judgment stay in the loop.

slides.title: AI accelerates thinking; it does not verify reality.
slides.subtitle: What AI Can and Cannot Do
bullets: Good at language, structure, options, summaries, and role-play.
Risky with facts, legal claims, market stats, and property details.
Human review is part of the workflow, not an optional step.
slides.notes: Talk track: Reinforce that the agent's license and judgment stay in the loop.

slides.title: Framework
slides.subtitle: What AI Can and Cannot Do
bullets: Good at language, structure, options, summaries, and role-play.
Risky with facts, legal claims, market stats, and property details.
Human review is part of the workflow, not an optional step.
slides.notes: Walk through the framework quickly, then move to the demo. The framework should feel usable, not academic.

slides.title: Live Practice
slides.subtitle: Compare a generic AI listing description with a revised version.
bullets: Circle risky claims in an AI-generated property description.
Debrief: what improved, what still needs human review?
slides.notes: Facilitate the exercise. Invite one example from the room if time allows. Keep the output short and client-safe.

slides.title: 3. AI for Client Communication
slides.subtitle: 25 minutes
bullets: Better context creates messages that sound more like the agent.
slides.notes: Keep the live demo short. The win is showing before/after quality.

slides.title: Better context creates messages that sound more like the agent.
slides.subtitle: AI for Client Communication
bullets: Audience: buyer, seller, lead, past client.
slides.notes: Talk track: Keep the live demo short. The win is showing before/after quality.

slides.title: Framework
slides.subtitle: AI for Client Communication
bullets: Audience: buyer, seller, lead, past client.
Goal: reply, clarity, appointment, next step.
slides.notes: Walk through the framework quickly, then move to the demo. The framework should feel usable, not academic.

slides.title: Live Practice
slides.subtitle: Draft a seller update after a quiet week of showings.
bullets: Rewrite a robotic text into a natural agent voice.
Debrief: what improved, what still needs human review?
slides.notes: Facilitate the exercise. Invite one example from the room if time allows. Keep the output short and client-safe.

slides.title: 4. AI for Lead Follow-Up
slides.subtitle: 20 minutes
bullets: Lead follow-up improves when it is fast, specific, and useful.
slides.notes: Mention that tools like GoHouse.ai can support lead context, then move on.

slides.title: Lead follow-up improves when it is fast, specific, and useful.
slides.subtitle: AI for Lead Follow-Up
bullets: Reference what the lead actually did or asked.
Offer value before asking for a meeting.
slides.notes: Talk track: Mention that tools like GoHouse.ai can support lead context, then move on.

slides.title: Framework
slides.subtitle: AI for Lead Follow-Up
bullets: Reference what the lead actually did or asked.
Offer value before asking for a meeting.
Save reusable prompts for common lead types.
slides.notes: Walk through the framework quickly, then move to the demo. The framework should feel usable, not academic.

slides.title: Live Practice
slides.subtitle: Create a response to a synthetic property inquiry lead.
bullets: Write a follow-up for a buyer who went silent.
Debrief: what improved, what still needs human review?
slides.notes: Facilitate the exercise. Invite one example from the room if time allows. Keep the output short and client-safe.

bullets: AI turns raw property details into usable marketing drafts.

slides.title: 7. Daily AI Workflow
slides.subtitle: 20 minutes
bullets: AI sticks when it is attached to the day, not treated as homework.
slides.notes: This is the behavioral close. Keep it simple and repeatable.

slides.title: AI sticks when it is attached to the day, not treated as homework.

slides.title: Framework
slides.subtitle: Daily AI Workflow
bullets: End of day: summarize notes and next steps.

slides.title: Live Practice
slides.subtitle: Build a sample day plan from a fictional agent task list.

Debrief: what improved, what still needs human review?

slides.title: 8. Safe and Professional AI Use
slides.subtitle: 15 minutes
bullets: The safest AI workflow is private, verified, compliant, and human.
slides.notes: End with confidence, not fear. Safety makes AI more usable.

slides.title: The safest AI workflow is private, verified, compliant, and human.
slides.subtitle: Safe and Professional AI Use
bullets: Do not paste sensitive client data into public tools.
Verify facts, stats, and property details.
Avoid Fair Housing risk in marketing language.
slides.notes: Talk track: End with confidence, not fear. Safety makes AI more usable.

slides.title: Framework
slides.subtitle: Safe and Professional AI Use
bullets: Do not paste sensitive client data into public tools.
Verify facts, stats, and property details.
Avoid Fair Housing risk in marketing language.
Keep the agent voice and final review.
slides.notes: Walk through the framework quickly, then move to the demo. The framework should feel usable, not academic.

slides.title: Live Practice
slides.subtitle: Review a risky generated neighborhood blurb.
bullets: Create a personal AI safety checklist.
Debrief: what improved, what still needs human review?
slides.notes: Facilitate the exercise. Invite one example from the room if time allows. Keep the output short and client-safe.

slides.title: What to Take Back to Your Business
slides.subtitle: Pick one workflow and make it repeatable.
bullets: Remove sensitive client information before prompting.
Provide verified facts and avoid asking AI to invent details.
Give AI audience, context, tone, format, and goal.
Review before sending anything to a client.
slides.notes: Use this slide to transition from learning into implementation.

slides.title: Your 7-Day Action Plan
slides.subtitle: Small, specific, and actually doable.
bullets: Write one reusable prompt for it.
Use it three times this week.
Save the best output as a template.
slides.notes: Have attendees write down one action before leaving. This is the close.

prompts: You are helping me as a Realtor draft a [message type] for [audience]. Context: [facts]. Tone: [tone]. Goal: [next step].
Rewrite this so it sounds like a helpful local Realtor, not a corporate robot: [paste draft].
Create three versions: concise text, warm email, and phone script.
Review this for factual claims, Fair Housing risk, and anything I should verify before sending.
checklist: Remove sensitive client information before prompting.
Provide verified facts and avoid asking AI to invent details.
Give AI audience, context, tone, format, and goal.
Review before sending anything to a client.
action_plan: Write one reusable prompt for it.
Use it three times this week.
Save the best output as a template.

title: AI Lead Conversion: How to Turn More Online Leads Into Appointments
promise: Use AI to turn internet lead activity into real conversations and appointments.
audience: Realtors who receive portal, IDX, website, open house, ad, or CRM leads.
objectives: Explain why online leads often fail to convert.
Write stronger first responses for different lead types.
Identify intent signals that suggest urgency.
Move from answering questions to setting appointments.

slides.title: What to Take Back to Your Business
slides.subtitle: Pick one workflow and make it repeatable.

slides.title: Your 7-Day Action Plan
slides.subtitle: Small, specific, and actually doable.

prompts: Create a first-response text for a [lead type]. Context: [lead activity]. Goal: start a conversation and move toward [appointment type].
Create a voicemail and matching text for this lead: [lead note]. Keep both brief and human.
Analyze these CRM notes and identify urgency, likely motivation, and best next message: [notes].
Role-play as a reluctant buyer lead. Push back realistically, then score my response.
checklist: Respond quickly, but use the lead's context.
Do not claim facts you have not verified.
Move toward a next step without forcing it.
Track response, contact, and appointment rates.
action_plan: Write a first-response prompt for that source.
Build text, email, voicemail, and call opener versions.
Track appointment conversion for one week.

title: AI for Online Lead Response and Nurture Campaigns
promise: Build follow-up systems that stay useful long after the first response.
audience: Realtors who want better structure after online leads enter the database.
objectives: Create first-response scripts for different lead types.
Segment leads before assigning nurture campaigns.
Re-engage old leads without sounding awkward.
Personalize follow-up using behavior and context.

slides.title: 6. Build a Seller Lead Nurture
slides.subtitle: 20 minutes
bullets: Seller nurture should build confidence before asking for the appointment.
slides.notes: Avoid pretending AI has appraised the home.

slides.title: Seller nurture should build confidence before asking for the appointment.
slides.subtitle: Build a Seller Lead Nurture

slides.title: Framework
slides.subtitle: Build a Seller Lead Nurture
slides.notes: Walk through the framework quickly, then move to the demo. The framework should feel usable, not academic.

slides.title: Live Practice
slides.subtitle: Create a 30-day campaign for a homeowner who requested a valuation.
bullets: Write a message that asks about timing without pressure.
Debrief: what improved, what still needs human review?
slides.notes: Facilitate the exercise. Invite one example from the room if time allows. Keep the output short and client-safe.

slides.title: 7. Re-Engage Old Leads
slides.subtitle: 15 minutes
bullets: Old leads need a fresh reason to reply, not guilt.
slides.notes: This is usually a favorite exercise because everyone has old leads.

slides.title: Old leads need a fresh reason to reply, not guilt.
slides.subtitle: Re-Engage Old Leads
slides.notes: Talk track: This is usually a favorite exercise because everyone has old leads.

slides.title: Framework
slides.subtitle: Re-Engage Old Leads
slides.notes: Walk through the framework quickly, then move to the demo. The framework should feel usable, not academic.

slides.title: Live Practice
slides.subtitle: Write reactivation messages for three stale lead scenarios.
bullets: Remove anything that sounds like blame.
Debrief: what improved, what still needs human review?
slides.notes: Facilitate the exercise. Invite one example from the room if time allows. Keep the output short and client-safe.

slides.title: 8. Behavior-Based Follow-Up
slides.subtitle: 10 minutes
bullets: Behavior gives the campaign its next useful reason to exist.
slides.notes: Tie lightly to systems that capture online behavior.

slides.title: Behavior gives the campaign its next useful reason to exist.
slides.subtitle: Behavior-Based Follow-Up
slides.notes: Talk track: Tie lightly to systems that capture online behavior.

slides.title: Framework
slides.subtitle: Behavior-Based Follow-Up
slides.notes: Walk through the framework quickly, then move to the demo. The framework should feel usable, not academic.

slides.title: Live Practice
slides.subtitle: Create a follow-up for a lead who returned three times in a week.
bullets: Choose one behavior trigger to implement first.
Debrief: what improved, what still needs human review?
slides.notes: Facilitate the exercise. Invite one example from the room if time allows. Keep the output short and client-safe.

slides.title: What to Take Back to Your Business
slides.subtitle: Pick one workflow and make it repeatable.
bullets: Do not send every lead the same campaign.
Make each touch useful and specific.
Vary channel, reason, and call to action.
Measure replies and appointments, not just sends.
slides.notes: Use this slide to transition from learning into implementation.

slides.title: Your 7-Day Action Plan
slides.subtitle: Small, specific, and actually doable.
bullets: Review campaign performance after two weeks.
slides.notes: Have attendees write down one action before leaving. This is the close.

prompts: Create a 7-day follow-up sequence for a [lead type]. Mix text, email, and call prompts. Avoid repeating the same message.
Segment these leads into active, warm, long-term, cold, and reactivation groups: [lead list].
Write a 30-day nurture campaign for a buyer interested in [area/property type] but not ready yet.
Rewrite this reactivation message so it sounds useful and not awkward: [message].
checklist: Do not send every lead the same campaign.
Make each touch useful and specific.
Vary channel, reason, and call to action.
Measure replies and appointments, not just sends.
action_plan: Review campaign performance after two weeks.
